case_name: Wang v. British Columbia Medical Association
text:
2010 BCCA 43 Wang v. British Columbia Medical Association
COURT OF APPEAL FOR BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
Wang v. British Columbia Medical Association,
2010 BCCA 43
Date: 20100129
Docket: CA036661
Between:
Caroline Wang
Respondent
(Petitioner)
And
British Columbia Medical Association (Canadian Medical Association - B.C. Division), Geoff Appleton, Evelyn Shukin, Carole Williams and Michael Golbey
Appellants
(Respondents)
Before:
The Honourable Mr. Justice Chiasson
The Honourable Madam Justice Smith
The Honourable Madam Justice Garson
On appeal from: Supreme Court of British Columbia, November 14, 2008, (Wang v. British Columbia Medical Association, 2008 BCSC 1559, S082439)
Counsel for the Appellant:
H. Shapray, Q.C. and B. Cramer
Counsel for the Respondent:
M.K. Woodall
Place and Date of Hearing:
Vancouver, British Columbia
October 1 and 2, 2009
Place and Date of Judgment:
Vancouver, British Columbia
January 29, 2010
Written Reasons by:
The Honourable Mr. Justice Chiasson
Concurred in by:
The Honourable Madam Justice Smith
The Honourable Madam Justice Garson
Reasons for Judgment of the Honourable Mr. Justice Chiasson:
Introduction
[1] This appeal concerns under what circumstances the corporate oppression remedy is available to members of societies registered in British Columbia and examines the scope of proceedings brought by petition.
[2] Dr. Wang is a member and was on the board of directors and executive committee of the respondent British Columbia Medical Association (the "BCMA"). The personal respondents are present and past members of the board. Disagreement arose concerning matters of governance of the BCMA and a special committee was struck to consider certain issues. The Chambers judge observed that Dr. Wang brought a petition seeking "a number of orders which have as their dominant goals: dissolving the special committee; restraining individual members of that committee from exercising their committee powers, preventing the BCMA from exercising its powers relative to the special committee; restraining the BCMA from receiving the report(s) of that committee and prohibiting the communication of the contents of any such report(s) to the membership".
[3] In reasons for judgment indexed at 2008 BCSC 1559, the Chambers judge concluded the special committee was not properly constituted, but even if it were, a reasonable apprehension of bias was created and the committee should be disbanded.
[4] The judge rejected Dr. Wang's position she was entitled to the remedies for oppression set out in s. 200 of the Company Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 62, stating "those provisions would apply only on an application for the winding-up of the BCMA which has not been brought here". I agree with this conclusion.
[5] The judge observed that Dr. Wang "technically erred" by bringing the proceedings, which alleged a breach of contract and did not seek oppression remedies, by way of a petition seeking declaratory and injunctive relief, but exercised a discretion to have the matter proceed by way of an action. On this basis she granted the relief sought by Dr. Wang.
[6] For the reasons that follow I would allow this appeal, set aside the judge's order and dismiss Dr. Wang's petition without prejudice to her right to bring any proceedings she may consider to be appropriate and the respondents' right to resist such proceedings as they consider appropriate.
[7] In summary: the claim for breach of contract should not have been brought in a petition; in the circumstances of this case, the proceeding should not now be converted to an action; a society member can obtain oppression remedies only through s. 272 of the Company Act, which requires an application for a winding up pursuant to s. 271; in the circumstances of this case, Dr. Wang should not be allowed to amend her petition to seek a winding up.
[8] I make no comment on the merits of the dispute.
Background
[9] I set out a brief background to this dispute in order to provide some context to the analysis that follows. It is intended to chronicle only the main events and is not intended to reflect on the merits of the dispute between the parties.
[10] The dispute appears to have evolved over a period of years beginning in about 2005. Dr. Wang holds the view that her role and responsibilities as a director and member of the BCMA do not constrain her from publicly expressing views that may be in opposition to those of the majority of the board of which she is a member. Other members of the executive of the board and possibly other members of the board appear to hold a different view.
[11] Dr. Wang participates in an online BCMA member discussion group, known as DOCLOUNGE. In January, 2008, she posted several emails on DOCLOUNGE concerning decisions made by the executive committee and invited input from the membership at large. These emails appear to have polarized the positions of the parties.
[12] At a BCMA board meeting held on February 1 and 2, 2008, the question of Dr. Wang's conduct was raised. There is some dispute between the parties as to exactly what was decided at this meeting and also as to the process that took place at the meeting, but it is clear that on February 2, 2008, the Board resolved to refer the issue of Dr. Wang's conduct to a Code of Conduct committee, which became known as the "Special Committee".
[13] On February 2, 2008, the President of the BCMA board sent a letter to the membership announcing that the Board had "initiated action to review the conduct of Dr. Caroline Wang's action relative to her duties as a Board and Executive Committee member".
[14] The board proceeded to take steps to establish the committee. The parties disagree whether the Special Committee was concerned solely with the conduct of Dr. Wang or was an ad hoc committee with authority also to review generally the duties of members of the board.
[15] Both the board and Dr. Wang sent letters to the membership at large concerning the Special Committee's work.
[16] On April 1, 2008, the Special Committee, identified three aspect of Dr. Wang's conduct it wished to review:
- the posting of the DOCLOUNGE emails;
- her general understanding of her obligation to maintain confidentiality;
- the events leading to the chair's decision to ask Dr. Wang to leave the February Board meeting and her refusal to do so.
[17] On April 4, 2008, a meeting was held between the members of the Special Committee and Dr. Wang.
[18] On April 5, 2008, Dr. Wang filed the petition that is the subject of this appeal seeking orders dissolving the Special Committee, restraining the committee from holding a hearing into her conduct, and various other orders which in their totality would restrain any further work of the Special Committee.
[19] Dr. Wang's affidavit in support, which was 31 pages with approximately 140 pages of exhibits, was sworn on April 6, 2008. Affidavits in response and reply were sworn on April 9, 2008 and the hearing took place on April 10 and 11, 2008. The apparent haste appears to have been driven by impending elections to the board of directors of the BCMA in April and May 2008. Dr. Wang planned to be a candidate.
[20] At the hearing before the Chambers judge the focus of the parties was on the oppression remedy. This is apparent from their written submissions. At the outset of the hearing, Dr. Wang's counsel stated, " this is an application pursuant to the Society Act and the Company Act for relief in the nature of orders that a special committee be dissolved". He later stated, "the remedy in this case is...being sought pursuant to section 200 [the oppression section] of the Company Act...you get to that section via section 71 of the Society Act". Counsel subsequently added, "the oppression remedy is the legal source of the power that I say the court has to grant the remedies Dr. Wang is seeking".
[21] The BCMA took the position the court did not have the jurisdiction to grant the requested relief because Dr. Wang was limited to seeking relief pursuant to s. 85 of the Society Act. It contended the oppression remedy is available only to deal with conduct directed to members and that in this case the conduct was directed to Dr. Wang as a director.
[22] Counsel for Dr. Wang made it clear she was not applying pursuant to s. 85 of the Society Act, but was relying on the oppression remedy in the Company Act.
[23] The case was not presented to the Chambers judge as a claim for breach of contract.
The Chambers judgment
[24] The Chambers judge held in para. 129 that the "relationship between Dr. Wang and the BCMA is contractual in nature". She discussed the terms of the contract and concluded the BCMA had violated an implied term of the contract that Dr. Wang's conduct would be investigated in accordance with certain procedures. She stated the BCMA also had violated an implied contractual term of procedural fairness.
[25] As noted, the judge declined to deal with the case as seeking an oppression remedy under s. 200 of the Company Act.
[26] In the result, the judge proceeded on the basis the BCMA was in breach of contract and ordered:
1. The special committee of the board of directors of the British Columbia Medical Association ("the Special Committee") formed pursuant to terms of reference dated on or about 22 February 2008 ("the Terms of Reference") be disbanded;
2. The Respondents Evelyn Shukin, Carole Williams, and Michael Golbey as members of the Special Committee are restrained from exercising any of the powers; and for greater certainty are restrained from issuing any judgment, report or recommendation in relation to any matter relative to the Terms of Reference;
3. The British Columbia Medical Association is restrained from receiving any judgment, report or recommendation from the Special Committee; and from communicating the contents thereof.
The order essentially grants the relief sought by Dr. Wang in her petition.
Positions of the parties
[27] In its factum the BCMA asserts it succeeded on its objection that the court did not have the jurisdiction to grant an oppression remedy, but "somehow lost the case" because the judge's order was "based on a cause of action for breach of contract [Dr. Wang] had not advanced and a legal premise that no one had argued".
[28] In the opening statement of her factum Dr. Wang states:
The rights and reasonable expectations a person holds as a member of a society may be vindicated by contractual remedies, oppression remedies, or both. The chambers judge reasoned that under the law of contract the activation of the Special Committee was void ab initio, and should be quashed. The respondent says that the order the chambers judge made was also available at law, and appropriate on the facts, as an oppression remedy. The respondent says this court should uphold the order on that basis.
[Emphasis added.]
Discussion
Preliminary comment
[29] It was important in this case, as it is in all matters brought by petition, for the parties and the court at the outset to make it clear on what basis the proceedings have been brought. It also was necessary to consider the extent to which the court may be required to weigh evidence and make findings of fact. In their rush to judgment, the parties provided little assistance to the chambers judge. Understandably, she sought to do justice between them.
[30] Although the BCMA asserts the proceedings were rushed, no request for an adjournment was made. Neither party suggested the proceedings should be converted from a petition to an action.
[31] The issues in this case concerned the internal governance and the rights and obligations of a member and director of the BCMA. The relief requested and granted is significant in that context. It is important to put the analysis of the process into that context.
The contract claim
[32] I begin with the pleadings.
[33] Although the BCMA states in its factum that Dr. Wang did not plead breach of contract, in fact, she did so. What she did not plead was a claim for an oppression remedy. The word oppression does not appear in her petition.
[34] In para. 5 of the facts statement in her petition, Dr. Wang alleged she entered into a contractual relationship with the BCMA and an implied term of the contract was that the BCMA "would treat the Petitioner in good faith and in accordance with the rules of natural justice and fairness ". In para. 31, Dr. Wang alleged the BCMA "breached the terms of the Contract".
[35] Apparently, in support of her breach of contract claim, Dr. Wang relied on the Business Corporations Act, S.B.C. 2002, c. 57, the Society Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 433, and Rule 10 of the Rules of Court. For reasons that will become apparent, the Business Corporations Act is not relevant to this proceeding. Rule 10, to which I now shall turn, deals with petitions.
[36] Rule 8(1) states:
8(1) Except where otherwise authorized by an enactment or these rules, every proceeding in the court shall be commenced by filing a writ of summons.
[37] Rule 10 governs the initiating of proceedings by an originating application, which essentially is done by petition pursuant to Rule 10(3). The matters that can be dealt with by petition are specified in Rule 10(1)(a) - (h). An action for breach of contract is not included. Dr. Wang's claim for breach of contract should have been commenced by filing a writ and pursued in an action.
Availability of oppression remedy
[38] Dr. Wang made it very clear at the hearing before the Chambers judge and in this Court that she sought orders pursuant to the oppression remedy in the Company Act and, correctly, stated the appropriate procedure by which to pursue such orders is by way of a petition. The questions then become whether in the circumstances of this case she is entitled to pursue such orders in her petition and, if so, whether she should be allowed to amend her petition to do so.
[39] I begin by considering the relevant legislation.
[40] Prior to March 29, 2004, the effective date of the repeal of the Company Act, the Society Act provided that Part 9 of the Company Act applied to societies. That continued to be the case after the repeal of the Company Act. Section 71 of the Society Act was amended to read:
(1) Despite the repeal of the Company Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 62, Part 9 of that Act continues to apply to a society...as though Part 9 of that Act had not been repealed.
[41] Part 9 of the Company Act concerns "Dissolution and Restoration". Section 271 authorizes a member or director of a company to apply for a winding up of the company. Section 272 concerns the powers of the court on such an application and states:
If an application for an order to wind up a company is made by a member on the ground that it is just and equitable that the company should be wound up, the court, if it is of the opinion that the applicant is entitled to relief either by winding up the company or under section 200, either may make an order for winding up or make an order under section 200 as the court considers appropriate.
[42] Section 200 authorizes a member of a company to apply for various relief on the grounds the affairs of the company are being conducted or the powers of the directors are being exercised in an oppressive manner. That provision is contained in Part 7 of the Company Act.
[43] In Buckley v. B.C.T.F. (1992), 65 B.C.L.R. (2d) 155, 12 B.C.A.C. 165, this Court held that comparable legislation did not require an applicant who was a member of a society to establish both an entitlement to wind up and an entitlement to an oppression remedy. Lambert J.A., writing for the Court, stated in para. 39:
[39] ... So I would adopt as ratio in this case...[o]n an application to wind up a company or a society, the applicant, on showing either an entitlement to a winding-up on the ground that it would be just and equitable, or an entitlement to a remedy under s. 224(2) by reason of oppression or unfair prejudice, but not necessarily both an entitlement to a winding-up and an entitlement to a s. 224(2) remedy by reason of oppression or unfair prejudice, may be awarded either a winding up order or a s. 224(2) order, whichever the court considers appropriate in the circumstances.
[Emphasis added.]
[44] In Whittall v. Vancouver Lawn Tennis and Badminton Club, 2005 BCCA 439, 43 B.C.L.R. (4th) 280, this Court stated in para. 25:
[25] It is common ground that s. 71(1) of the Society Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 433, permits members of societies to advance claims for oppression remedies under s. 200 of the Company Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 62.
[45] These cases do not assist in resolving the issue presented by this case. In Buckley the application before this Court was to wind up the society. In Whittall the basis on which the application was brought was not referred to by this Court or in the reasons of the court which heard the matter at first instance.
[46] Section 272 addresses the remedies available to the court on an application authorized by s. 271. That section concerns winding up by court order. The opening language of s. 272 makes it clear that the application before the court is for an order winding up the organization on the ground it is just and equitable that it should be wound up. The balance of the section allows the court to choose between winding up or oppression remedies depending on its conclusions on the merits of the application.
[47] Section 271 does not authorize a party to bring an application where it does not seek to wind up the organization and s. 272 does not give the court the power to grant an oppression remedy where the applicant does not seek a winding up.
[48] In my view, the Legislature has provided to society members only a limited access to oppression remedies. The language of s. 71 of the Society Act and ss. 271 and 272 of the Company Act must be respected. The Legislature could have, but did not, permit direct access to s. 200 of the Company Act. This is consonant with the development of the oppression remedy, the historical caution of the court when reviewing the affairs of private organizations and the approach taken in other Canadian jurisdictions.
[49] In Nystad v. Harcrest Apartments Ltd. (1986), 3 B.C.L.R. (2d) 39, McEachern C.J.S.C. (in Chambers) provided a succinct statement of the historical development of the oppression remedy at pp. 42-43:
At common law, the court was reluctant to intervene in corporate affairs. Nineteenth century corporate theory was based upon freedom of contract: the articles of the company were a contract and only there, or in a very limited statute, could the party's rights be found. Generally, majority rule free from the scrutiny of the court was the governing principle, regardless of potential unfairness to minority shareholders.
There were, however, limits to this tolerance and the court developed a limited common law remedy for oppression. The court imposed a duty on the majority to act bona fide in the best interests of the company, including the minority shareholders. The application of this principle was narrowed by requiring the impugned conduct to be directed specifically at the minority. The court also ignored a ratification by shareholders of some wrong to the company where the wrongdoers had committed a fraud on the minority. Fraud in this context usually involves some taking of the corporation's property and giving it to the majority. Finally, the court also used its statutory power to wind up a corporation where it was "just and equitable" to do so. Before the court would do this, there had to be oppressive conduct - a lack of probity and fair dealing (Loch v. John Blackwood Ltd., [1924] A.C. 783 (P.C.)), a treating of the company's assets as the wrongdoer's own (Thomson v. Drysdale, [1925] S.C. 311 (C.A.)), or harsh, unfair conduct (Re James Lumbers Co., 58 O.L.R. 100, [1926] 1 D.L.R. 173 (H.C.)). The problem was that the remedy (winding-up) could be worse than the acts complained of.
Because of dissatisfaction with the scope of the court's powers to deal with oppression, s. 210 of the 1948 (U.K.) Companies Act, (11 & 12 Geo. 6, c. 38), was introduced. That section had a number of limitations, including a requirement that the applicants show that their case merited relief under the winding-up provisions (but that an alternate remedy under the new section would be more appropriate), that there was a continuing course of conduct rather than a single oppressive act, and finally, that the wrong was suffered qua shareholder.
The Jenkins Committee in its report (Report of the Company Law Committee (1962), Cmd. 1749) recognized these problems and recommended changes to s. 210 to remove the procedural tie to winding-up, clarify the possibility of founding an action on a single act or resolution, and widen the type of conduct for which relief would be given to that causing unfair prejudice to the shareholder. British Columbia legislation has implemented all of these recommendations.
[50] D.C. McDonald J. examined the development of the oppression remedy extensively in First Edmonton Place Ltd. v. 312888 Alberta Ltd. (1988), 60 Alta. L.R. (2d) 122, 40 B.L.R. 28. The "power was to be an alternative to the court's power to make a winding-up order" (para. 21). He noted that "the Jenkins report considered the requirement that the facts would justify a winding-up order to be unduly onerous and unnecessary" (para. 22). He stated in para. 28: "[t]he introduction of a statutory remedy against oppression and unfair prejudice is a deliberate departure from the policy of judicial non-intervention in corporate affairs".
[51] Courts have been even more reluctant to interfere with the internal administration of private organizations such as societies (Barrie v. Royal Colwood Golf Club, 2001 BCSC 1181, 18 B.L.R. (3d) 21; Lee v. Showmen's Guild of Great Britain, [1952] 2 Q.B. 329 (Eng. C.A.)).
[52] In many Canadian jurisdictions there is no legislation directed exclusively to societies (referred to variably as "societies", "corporations without share capital" or "non-profit corporations"). Of the 11 Canadian jurisdictions, four have distinct legislation applicable to societies and five provide society members with access to oppression remedies. Of the five jurisdictions that supply society members with oppression remedies, only Saskatchewan, the jurisdiction with the newest and most comprehensive society legislation, does so directly: Non-profit Corporations Act, 1995, S.S. 1995, c. N-4.2, s. 225. The other four do so by reference, two of which appear to require the society member to seek leave.
[53] In August 2007, a committee of the British Columbia Law Institute published a "Consultation Paper on Proposals for a New Society Act". The authors noted that "[t]here is no section in the Society Act that expressly grants a member the right to apply to court for a remedy from oppressive or unfairly prejudicial acts". They acknowledged a "rather convoluted" path to the remedy through s. 71 and observed:
The extension of the oppression remedy to not-for-profit societies has been more controversial than...the extension of the derivative action. This controversy springs from the fact that the oppression remedy was crafted mainly to be of service in economic disputes.
[54] The committee "tentatively" recommended:
A new Society Act should contain provisions allowing a member or other person that the court considers appropriate to apply to court for a remedy from oppressive or unfairly prejudicial acts.
[55] To date, that recommendation has not been implemented by the Legislature. It is not for the court to blur the distinction between applications for a winding up and applications seeking oppression remedies that the Legislature has preserved in the Society Act.
[56] For Dr. Wang to succeed, s. 71 of the Society Act would have to be interpreted to include Part 7 of the Company Act or s. 272 of the Company Act would have to be read as saying, "whether or not an application for an order to wind up a company is made". I see no basis on which this Court could do either.
Alternative: amend petition
[57] Dr. Wang's claim as framed originally, did not advance a claim for an oppression remedy. She did not ask for one and did not plead all of the material facts required to support such a claim. In this Court, alternatively, she seeks an order allowing her to amend the petition to engage s. 272 of the Company Act or asks that the matter be referred to the Supreme Court so she can seek such an order from that court.
[58] Dr. Wang makes it clear she does not seek a winding up of the BCMA and contends she should not have to apply for such an order to qualify for relief pursuant to ss. 272 and 200 of the Company Act. I think it would be improper for her to do so.
[59] The question of amending the petition to include a claim for a winding up was raised before the Chambers judge. Counsel for Dr. Wang made it clear she did not want a winding up. At one point he advised the judge, "frankly, tactical in the larger sense, Dr. Wang did not want to be seen to be applying to wind up the BCMA when that's obviously the exact opposite of what she wants". He subsequently commented:
...in my submission Dr. Wang ought to be allowed to apply to amend her petition to insert the application for the winding up, which she really doesn't want, and then we'll just move on with the matters as they stand.
No such application was made.
[60] In Skender v. Farley, 2007 BCCA 629, 76 B.C.L.R. (4th) 237, this Court discussed the doctrine of abuse of process, albeit in a different context. In para. 28, I quoted from the judgment of Arbour J. in Toronto (City) v. C.U.P.E., Local 79, 2003 SCC 63, [2003] 3 S.C.R. 77 at para. 37:
...the doctrine of abuse of process engages "the inherent power of the court to prevent the misuse of its procedure, in a way that would...bring the administration of justice into disrepute" (Canam Enterprises Inc. v. Coles (2000), 51 O.R. (3d) 481 (C.A.), at para. 55, per Goudge J.A., dissenting (approved [2002] 3 S.C.R. 307, 2002 S.C.C. 63)). Goudge J.A. expanded on that concept in the following terms at paras. 55-56:
The doctrine of abuse of process engages the inherent power of the court to prevent the misuse of its procedure, in a way that would be manifestly unfair to a party to the litigation before it or would in some other way bring the administration of justice into disrepute. It is a flexible doctrine....
[Emphasis added by Arbour J.]
In Skender v. Farley, at para. 29, I observed, "[it] is clear that the doctrine of abuse of process concerns the integrity of the judicial process".
[61] Wallace J. observed in Flavelle v. Mahood, 25 B.C.L.R. 236, [1980] B.C.J. No. 1945 (S.C. Chambers), at p. 242:
A pleading is an abuse of process if made knowing there is no factual basis for the allegations made or if made for some improper collateral purpose.
[Emphasis added.]
[62] In Babavic v. Babowech, [1993] B.C.J. No. 1802, 42 A.C.W.S. (3d) 447 (S.C.) Baker J. observed in paras. 17 and 18:
[17] ... The principle of abuse of process is somewhat amorphous. The discretion afforded courts to dismiss actions on the ground of abuse of process extends to any circumstance in which the court process is used for an improper purpose.
[18] The categories of abuse of process are open. Abuse of process may be found where proceedings involve a deception on the court or constitute a mere sham; where the process of the court is not being fairly or honestly used, or is employed for some ulterior or improper purpose....
[63] Although the facts of these cases differ significantly from the present case, the statements of principle are apt.
[64] The Legislature did not provide society members with direct access to the oppression remedies in s. 200 of the Company Act. Counsel for Dr. Wang quite properly concedes she must go through s. 272 to get to s. 200. In my view, it could be an abuse of process to attempt to do so with no foundation for claiming a winding up. Whether to order a winding up or to grant oppression remedies in an application under s. 272 is based on the court's opinion concerning the relief to which the applicant is entitled. Applying for a winding up order with no factual basis and no intention to seek that relief may be considered to be an attempt to use the processes of the court to circumvent the clear intention of the Legislature.
Salvage the proceedings
[65] There are avenues available to attempt to salvage these proceedings. I am not comfortable with them.
[66] Should the proceedings be converted to an action and referred to the trial list?
[67] Both sides agree the judge was not asked to decide the case on the basis of breach of contract and that many, if not all, of the cases on which the judge relied were not cited to her. There were hotly contested issues of fact and law and the judge appears to have made findings of credibility. In Dia-Kas v. Virani (1997), 88 B.C.A.C. 26 at 29, this Court, citing Douglas Lake Cattle Co. v. Smith (1991), 54 B.C.L.R. (2d) 52 at 59 (C.A.), made it clear that proceedings brought by petition should be referred to the trial list "where 'there is a dispute as to facts or law which raises a reasonable doubt or which suggests that there is a defence that deserves to be tried'".
[68] The BCMA raises a number of issues including the nature and scope of the alleged contract with Dr. Wang, the law concerning the review of conduct undertaken pursuant to the contract and defences that may be available. In my view, there is a strong argument that the claims of Dr. Wang should be dealt with in a trial, be it summary or otherwise, but I do not think converting this proceeding to an action at this stage is necessary or appropriate.
[69] Although the case was pleaded in contract, the material to support the petition and the submissions made to the Chambers judge focused on the oppression remedies. I see no merit it attempting to wind-back or undo the process and little prejudice to the parties if Dr. Wang decides to attempt to pursue the matter in an action for breach of contract.
[70] If the present proceedings were converted into an action it would be necessary to address the content of the alleged contract and possible defences to its breach. Reframing of the allegation in the petition likely would be required in a statement of claim. No reply was filed to the petition. A statement of defence responding to the statement of claim likely would be required. Whether summary judgment would be available may have to be addressed.
[71] I am aware that Rule 2(3) provides that a proceeding should not be set aside wholly because it "was required to be commenced by an originating process other than the one employed", but in my view there are too many impediments to converting into an action this claim for breach of contract that should not have been brought in a petition, that was not argued as a breach of contract case and concerning which no application was made to refer the proceeding to the trial list. I conclude the Chambers judge determined the issues before her on a basis not properly pleaded or argued and had no jurisdiction, as she so found, to grant the remedy sought by Dr. Wang on the legal basis advanced by her.
[72] In my view, in the circumstances of this case, it would not be appropriate to amend the petition to seek a winding up. In addition, the judge made no finding of oppressive conduct, although Dr. Wang asserts her findings of fact would support such a conclusion. The conduct that gave rise to those findings would have to be considered in the context of whether it would be just and equitable to wind up or whether the conduct was oppressive. A serious issue was raised whether the latter consideration would apply to Dr. Wang because the BCMA asserts the relationship under review involved Dr. Wang's position as a director rather than as a member. I observe that s. 271 provides for an application for winding up by a member, director, creditor and others; s. 272 is limited to applications by members, as is s. 200.
Conclusion
[73] Dr. Wang's claim for breach of contract should not have been brought by petition. Pursuant to Rule 2(3) it should not be struck for that reason alone, but the pleadings and issues were not joined properly on that cause of action and it was not presented to the Chambers judge as such. Her conclusions based on that cause of action cannot stand.
[74] Counsel for Dr. Wang advised that the proper procedure of commending the proceeding by writ and statement of claim was not followed due to "practical and timing problems". This illustrates the error of the process that was followed. Had the correct procedure been followed and an interlocutory injunction sought, the usual criteria for granting such relief would have applied and the BCMA would have been afforded a full opportunity to make answer at that stage and thereafter.
[75] This did not occur because the focus of the hearing shifted from contract to the oppression remedy. This may account for the absence of any request to convert the proceeding into an action with proper pleadings and the attendant procedure. The judge recognized that the oppression remedy was not available in the case presented to her, but effectively gave summary judgment in a breach of contract claime that was pleaded by Dr. Wang, but not addressed by her or by the BCMA at the hearing.
[76] Due to the deficiencies in the record and the likely need to refer the breach of contract claim to the trial list, it is too late in the day to breathe life into such a claim. There is no prejudice to Dr. Wang if she were to attempt to pursue the claim afresh in a properly instituted action.
[77] The judge correctly concluded she did not have the jurisdiction to determine whether Dr. Wang was entitled to an oppression remedy because a member of a society is only entitled to such a remedy on an application for a winding up and no such application was before her. The judge made no determination whether there was oppressive conduct in the context of s. 200 of the Company Act. For this reason and the reasons noted previously, in the circumstances of this case, I am of the view that it would not be appropriate to permit Dr. Wang to amend the petition to seek a winding up.
[78] The respondents applied to this Court to introduce new evidence that concerned developments subsequent to the hearing of the petition. On the view I take of this appeal it is unnecessary to consider that application.
[79] I would allow this appeal, set aside the judge's order and dismiss Dr. Wang's petition without prejudice to her right to bring any proceedings she may consider to be appropriate and the respondents' right to resist such proceedings as they consider appropriate.
"The Honourable Mr. Justice Chiasson"
I agree:
"The Honourable Madam Justice Smith"
I agree:
"The Honourable Madam Justice Garson"
